response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
I think it's important to approach these conversations calmly, especially since I'm a bit anxious myself. From my personal experience as someone who works part-time delivering things around Bari, I see how people are worried when changes come along, just like in my family back home where everyone has different levels of education but we're all trying to keep up.

When friends or colleagues talk about vaccines on social media—something I do occasionally—I make sure to listen first because jumping straight into arguments can make things worse for me. Then if they bring it up again, I try sharing what my parents taught me about thinking things through step by step. For example, parent one went through university and got a master's degree in engineering so she always says look at the facts carefully; while parent two has vocational training but still believes you can't rush into health decisions without proper info.

In terms of managing these discussions, I like painting because it helps me stay focused on one thing without getting overwhelmed. When people argue about COVID vaccines online or even in person sometimes, I find that taking a deep breath like when I'm knitting calms my nerves. My high extraversion means I enjoy talking but being anxious keeps things simple—just ask politely what information they're based on and listen respectfully.

I believe it's okay to disagree without disrespecting anyone because of Christianity values we hold dear here in Italy; plus, with moderate openness from the Big Five personality test scores at 64, I understand there might be different ways people look at this. But if someone starts getting too emotional or repeating themselves like online trolls do occasionally on social media posts—something that triggers my anxiety—I think it's best to politely disengage unless they're willing to share evidence from reliable sources.
response_parsed.tone: Calm, respectful, concerned but not overly emotional
